facts.label: THE GOLDEN TEAM
facts.story: Between 1950 and 1954, Hungary's Aranycsapat went 31 matches unbeaten, the cherry-red shirts of Ferenc Puskás, Sándor Kocsis, and Nándor Hidegkuti redefining how football could be played. Their signature win came in November 1953, when they thrashed England 6–3 at Wembley — the first defeat by a non-British team on English soil — a result so shocking the British press called it 'the Match of the Century.' Six months later, they were World Cup favorites.

facts.label: THE BATTLE OF BERNE
facts.story: Hungary's 1954 quarter-final against Brazil in Bern turned into one of football's ugliest nights. English referee Arthur Ellis handed out four cautions and three red cards as tackles turned to punches; József Bozsik and Brazil's Nílton Santos brawled after a foul and were both sent off, and Brazilian officials stormed the pitch after a disputed penalty. Hungary won 4–2, but the match became known as the 'Battle of Berne' — remembered for chaos, not football, in a tournament they'd go on to lose in the final.

1934.headline: Toldi's Bright Debut

annotations.label: TOLDI'S OPENING SALVO
annotations.story: Géza Toldi announced Hungary's World Cup arrival with two goals inside an hour against Egypt in Naples, the tournament's opener for both sides. Four days later Austria's Wunderteam ended the run 2–1 in Bologna; György Sárosi's 60th-minute reply arrived too late to save a debut that promised more than one match delivered.

annotations.label: NO NAMES, NO LOGOS
annotations.story: Hungary took the field in unadorned cherry red — heavy cotton, a laced V-collar, no sponsor mark, no name on the back, just the national crest stitched plain over the heart. It was a uniform built for function, not marketing, decades before shirts became billboards.

annotations.label: FOOTBALL UNDER IL DUCE
annotations.story: Mussolini's Italy hosted the 1934 tournament as a showcase for Fascist prestige, stadiums draped in banners and officials under pressure to deliver an Italian title. Hungary arrived as outsiders to that theater, their brief run through Naples and Bologna a footnote to a tournament remembered as much for politics as football.

1934.homeColors: red shirt, white shorts, green socks
1934.story: Hungary's first-ever World Cup match ended in celebration: Géza Toldi's brace, plus goals from Pál Teleki and Jenő Vincze, sank Egypt 4–2 in Naples. Four days later in Bologna, Austria's vaunted Wunderteam proved a tougher out, edging Hungary 2–1 in the quarter-finals despite György Sárosi's late reply — a promising debut cut short.

1938.headline: So Close To Gold
1938.homeColors: red shirt, white shorts, green socks
1938.story: Hungary steamrolled to the final — 6–0 over the Dutch East Indies, then wins over Switzerland and Sweden, with György Sárosi and Gyula Zsengellér scoring at will. In Paris's Stade Olympique de Colombes, Italy's Silvio Piola and Gino Colaussi were too much; Pál Titkos and Sárosi pulled goals back, but the final finished 4–2, Hungary's first runner-up medal.

matches.scorers: Kohut 13', Toldi 15', Sárosi 28', Zsengellér 35', Zsengellér 76', Sárosi 89'
Jacobsson OG 19', Titkos 37', Zsengellér 39', Sárosi 65', Zsengellér 85'

1954.headline: The Golden Team's Heartbreak
1954.homeColors: red shirt, white shorts, green socks

imageCredit.game: Hungary's Golden Team squad, 1954 FIFA World Cup — CC BY-SA 3.0 / Wikimedia Commons

1954.story: Sándor Kocsis scored eleven goals — still a Hungary record — as the Aranycsapat crushed South Korea 9–0, West Germany 8–3, and Brazil 4–2 in the brutal 'Battle of Berne.' Extra time beat Uruguay in the semi-final. Then, in the final, West Germany overturned a 2–0 deficit to win 3–2, ending a 31-game unbeaten run at the worst possible moment.

matches.scorers: Puskás 12', Lantos 18', Kocsis 24', Kocsis 36', Kocsis 50', Czibor 59', Palotás 75', Palotás 83', Puskás 89'
Kocsis 3', Puskás 17', Kocsis 21', Hidegkuti 52', Hidegkuti 54', Kocsis 69', Tóth 75', Kocsis 78'

1958.headline: Wales Twice, No Escape
1958.homeColors: red shirt, white shorts, green socks
1958.story: József Bozsik's equalizer salvaged a 1–1 draw with Wales, and Lajos Tichy's brace routed Mexico 4–0, but a 2–1 loss to Sweden forced a playoff rematch with Wales. Tichy scored again, but it wasn't enough — a 2–1 defeat sent Hungary home in the group stage as the Golden Team's era neared its end.

1962.headline: Tichy's Team, One Round Short
1962.homeColors: red shirt, white shorts, green socks
1962.story: Flórián Albert announced himself with a hat-trick in a 6–1 rout of Bulgaria, and Lajos Tichy's goals in a win over England and a scoreless draw with Argentina carried Hungary into the knockouts. But Czechoslovakia's quarter-final defense held firm, a 1–0 loss in Rancagua ending Hungary's run just short of the final four.

matches.scorers: Albert 1', Albert 6', Tichy 8', Solymosi 12', Albert 53', Tichy 70'

1966.headline: Bene Shines, Soviets End It
1966.homeColors: red shirt, white shorts, green socks
1966.story: Ferenc Bene scored in every match Hungary played, but a 3–1 opening loss to Portugal still stung. Wins over two-time defending champions Brazil and over Bulgaria carried Hungary into the quarter-finals, where the Soviet Union's defense held after Bene's early goal, a 2–1 defeat at Roker Park ending Hungary's last deep World Cup run.

1978.headline: Three Games, Three Losses
1978.homeColors: red shirt, white shorts, green socks
1978.story: Károly Csapó's goal wasn't enough against Argentina, András Tóth pulled one back in a 3–1 loss to Italy, and Sándor Zombori's late strike couldn't save Hungary from France in the finale. A rebuilding squad, without its 1966 core, exited Group 1 without a single point.

1982.headline: Kiss's Ten-Goal Explosion
1982.homeColors: red shirt, white shorts, green socks
1982.story: László Kiss came off the bench to score a hat-trick — still the fastest in World Cup history — in a 10–1 demolition of El Salvador, the tournament's biggest-ever win. Diego Maradona's Argentina answered with a 4–1 defeat, and a draw with Belgium wasn't enough; Hungary's group-stage exit couldn't dim Kiss's record night.

matches.scorers: Nyilasi 4', Pölöskei 11', Fazekas 23', Tóth 50', Fazekas 54', Kiss 69', Szentes 70', Kiss 72', Kiss 76', Nyilasi 83'

1986.headline: One Win, Long Goodbye
1986.homeColors: red shirt, white shorts, green socks
1986.story: The Soviet Union's 6–0 rout in the opener was Hungary's heaviest World Cup defeat, but Márton Esterházy and Lajos Détári salvaged a 2–0 win over Canada. France closed the book 3–0 in León, and Hungary hasn't returned to a World Cup since — a drought now stretching decades.
